title: The Sui Reunification
bullets: Yang Jian — a northern aristocrat of mixed Chinese-Xianbei background who already held both a Chinese surname and a Xianbei one — usurped the Northern Zhou throne in 581 and reunified China in 589 by crossing the Yangtze with 518,000 troops and finding the Chen emperor hiding in a palace well with two concubines.

As Emperor Wen of Sui, he built an entirely new capital at Daxing on a 32-square-mile grid plan that would later become Tang Chang'an and serve as the architectural template for Japanese Nara, Heian-kyō (Kyoto), and Korean capitals.

The Kaihuang Code of 583, which reduced capital offenses and distinguished intentional from accidental crimes in 500 articles, became the foundation of the Tang Code and through it of Japanese, Korean, and Vietnamese law.

Emperor Yang built the Grand Canal from Hangzhou to the area of modern Beijing — over 1,000 miles of artificial waterway, dug in six years by millions of corvée laborers, with very high mortality, and still in use today as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Emperor Yang's three invasions of the Korean kingdom of Goguryeo were catastrophic; the 612 campaign mobilized over a million troops and Korean general Eulji Mundeok destroyed the Sui army at the Salsu River, with only 2,700 of 305,000 returning across the Yalu.
By 618 the Sui had collapsed in nationwide rebellion; Emperor Yang was strangled by his own palace guards at Jiangdu and the Sui general Li Yuan seized Chang'an to proclaim the Tang dynasty.

The Sui's 37-year arc almost exactly mirrors the Qin dynasty's earlier short, harsh reunifying dynasty; the Tang founders explicitly studied the Sui collapse as a guide to what not to do, the way the Han had studied the Qin's mistakes.

title: The Tang Founding & High Tang
bullets: Li Yuan founded the Tang in 618; his middle son Li Shimin resolved the succession with the Xuanwu Gate Incident of July 626, ambushing and personally shooting his Crown Prince brother dead at the northern gate of the Chang'an palace.

As Emperor Taizong, Li Shimin's Zhenguan reign (627–649) became the gold standard against which all subsequent Chinese reigns were measured — defeating the Eastern Turkic Khaganate, refining the equal-field land system, and being acclaimed Heavenly Khan by the steppe peoples.

Wei Zheng was the formerly hostile advisor whom Taizong tolerated decades of public criticism from; the famous Taizong line at his death — 'I have lost a mirror' — became the model for the Confucian advisor-emperor relationship for the next 1,200 years.

Wu Zetian became the only woman in Chinese history to formally hold the title of Huangdi in her own name, founding the brief Zhou dynasty in 690 at age 66 and ruling effectively until forced to abdicate at 81 in 705.

She invented around twelve new Chinese characters during her reign — including a new character for her own name combining 'sun' and 'moon' above 'sky' — and her tomb's Wordless Stele was deliberately left blank, with her legacy left for history to judge.

Tang military expansion peaked under Su Dingfang's destruction of the Western Turkic Khaganate in 657, extending Chinese control across the Tarim Basin to the borders of Persia — the greatest territorial reach of any Chinese dynasty.

Emperor Xuanzong's Kaiyuan era (713–741) is what later Chinese tradition celebrates as the High Tang — the absolute peak in poetry, music, and territorial reach, before the same emperor's later decades produced the conditions for the An Lushan Rebellion.

title: The Cosmopolitan Capital
bullets: Tang Chang'an covered approximately 32 square miles with over a million residents inside its walls — about seven times the size of contemporary Constantinople and probably the largest city the world had ever seen up to that point.

Persian, Sogdian, Arab, Indian, Korean, and Japanese communities lived in their own quarters in Chang'an, with Zoroastrian fire temples, Nestorian Christian churches, Manichaean shrines, and Islamic mosques operating alongside Buddhist and Daoist establishments.

Sogdian merchants from Central Asia ran most of the Silk Road caravan trade and Sogdian served as the lingua franca of overland Eurasian commerce — Sogdian-language documents have been found from China to the Black Sea.

Chan Buddhism crystallized as a distinctly Chinese school during the Tang, emphasizing meditation and sudden enlightenment; transmitted to Korea as Seon, Japan as Zen, and Vietnam as Thiền, it would shape East Asian aesthetics for a thousand years.

Li Bai is the wild Daoist 'Immortal Poet' whose Quiet Night Thought is probably the most widely memorized Chinese poem ever written; legend says he drowned trying to embrace the moon's reflection in a river while drunk.

Du Fu is the disciplined Confucian 'Poet Sage' whose Spring View, written in 757 from rebel-occupied Chang'an, opens with one of the most quoted lines in Chinese literature: the country is broken, the mountains and rivers remain.

The composing of regulated verse was a required component of the jinshi examination, which is why every educated Tang man practiced poetry from his teenage years and the Complete Tang Poetry of 1705 contains over 48,000 surviving poems by more than 2,200 authors.

Tang sancai three-color glazed camel and merchant figurines preserved a remarkable visual record of the Silk Road era; Tang export ceramics have been found across Eurasia, and the Belitung shipwreck of c. 830 carried over 60,000 pieces.

title: The An Lushan Rebellion and the Late Tang
bullets: An Lushan was a 330-pound Sogdian-Turkic frontier general who entertained Emperor Xuanzong's court with whirling dances and whom Xuanzong reportedly trusted so completely he would have believed An Lushan if he said the sun and moon had collided.

An Lushan controlled three of the seven major Tang frontier military commands with over 160,000 professional troops and rebelled in December 755; he captured Luoyang within weeks and Chang'an by mid-756.

Emperor Xuanzong fled west toward Sichuan; at Mawei Station his guards mutinied and demanded the execution of his consort Yang Guifei, who was strangled with a silk cord by the chief eunuch Gao Lishi — one of the great tragic love stories in Chinese literature.

The eight-year rebellion killed an estimated 13 to 36 million people based on Tang census records before and after — possibly the deadliest single conflict before the 20th century, and decisively breaking the central Tang state's authority.

An Lushan went blind and was murdered by his own son in 757; the rebellion fragmented into a chain of patricides and assassinations before finally collapsing in 763, with Uyghur cavalry as the decisive military aid.

Real power after 763 shifted permanently to the regional jiedushi military commissioners, who collected their own taxes, raised their own armies, and passed their offices father-to-son with only nominal loyalty to the Tang court.

Emperor Wuzong's Great Anti-Buddhist Persecution of 845 destroyed 4,600 monasteries and 40,000 shrines, forcibly laicized 260,000 monks and nuns, and melted bronze Buddhas into coins — Buddhist institutional power in China was permanently reduced and only Chan recovered fully.

The Diamond Sutra of May 11, 868, recovered from the Mogao Caves at Dunhuang in 1900 and now in the British Library, is the world's oldest dated complete printed book — six centuries before Gutenberg's first European printing.

The Huang Chao Rebellion of 875–884 sacked Guangzhou (with reports of 120,000 foreign merchants massacred), captured Chang'an in 880, and finished the Tang as a functioning state; the warlord Zhu Wen formally deposed the last Tang emperor in 907.

title: The Five Dynasties & Song Founding
bullets: The Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period of 907–960 saw five short-lived northern dynasties (averaging just 13 years each) succeed each other, each overthrown by its own military commanders, with ten more stable kingdoms dividing the south.

The most consequential event of the period was the Loss of the Sixteen Prefectures in 938, when warlord Shi Jingtang ceded the area around modern Beijing — including all the natural mountain-pass defenses against steppe invasion — to the Khitan Liao in exchange for military support.

The Song would spend its entire 167-year northern existence trying to recover these prefectures and would never succeed; the Jurchen conquest of 1127 and the Mongol conquest of the 13th century both poured through these passes.

Zhao Kuangyin, senior general of the Later Zhou, was 'spontaneously' acclaimed emperor by his troops at Chenqiao Station in February 960 — a probably staged military coup that established the Song dynasty.

Within a year of taking the throne as Emperor Taizu, he hosted his senior generals at a banquet and famously offered them generous pensions and lifetime estates in exchange for surrendering their commands — the 'exchanging military power for wine cups' policy that defined Song governance.

Taizu systematically subordinated the military to civilian bureaucratic control, expanded the civil service examination quotas dramatically, and built history's most bureaucratic state — a constitutional shift that would shape every subsequent Chinese dynasty.

The Song reunification of the south (960–979) was conducted largely through diplomacy and negotiated submission rather than warfare, allowing the dynasty to begin its prosperity from a relatively undamaged starting position.

title: The Northern Song
bullets: Kaifeng broke from the walled-ward template of Tang Chang'an with open shop-lined streets, restaurants, and night markets operating past midnight — the most commercial Chinese city ever built and the world's most populous by 1000 CE.

Wang Anshi's New Policies of 1069–1076 introduced government loans to peasant farmers, state trading bureaus, the baojia militia system, and exam reforms emphasizing practical policy — and triggered a half-century of bitter factional warfare with conservatives led by Sima Guang.

Sima Guang spent his years out of power compiling the Zizhi Tongjian, a 294-volume chronological history of China covering 1,362 years from 403 BCE to 959 CE, which became the second most influential work of Chinese historiography after Sima Qian's Records of the Grand Historian.

The Wujing Zongyao of 1044 contains the earliest known weaponized gunpowder formulas — specific proportions of saltpeter, sulfur, and charcoal — alongside recipes for incendiary bombs, fire arrows, fire lances, thunderclap bombs, and signal rockets.

Bi Sheng invented ceramic movable type printing around 1040, four centuries before Gutenberg; Shen Kuo described the process in detail in his 1088 Dream Pool Essays, without which Bi Sheng's invention might be lost to history.

The Song government issued the world's first state-backed paper money — the jiaozi — in 1024, with multi-color printing to fight counterfeiting and three-year circulation periods to control the money supply, seven centuries before any European state issued paper currency.

Shen Kuo described floating-needle compasses for navigation in the Dream Pool Essays of 1088 and noted magnetic declination — the slight offset between magnetic north and true north — about four centuries before Christopher Columbus rediscovered the same observation in 1492.

Song iron production reached approximately 125,000 tons annually by 1078, exceeding England's iron output at the start of the Industrial Revolution and using coke-fired blast furnaces six centuries before Abraham Darby developed the same technique in Europe in 1709.

Champa rice imported from central Vietnam in 1012 enabled double-cropping and roughly doubled rice production; China's population grew from 60 million in 960 to over 100 million by 1100 — the first society in human history to surpass that threshold.

Emperor Huizong invented the elegant Slender Gold calligraphic script and was one of China's greatest artists, but his alliance with the Jurchen against the Khitan Liao in 1115 ended in the catastrophe of 1127, when both Huizong and his son were captured and marched north into permanent captivity.

title: The Jurchen Catastrophe and the Southern Song
bullets: The Jingkang Incident of 1127 saw Jurchen Jin armies capture Kaifeng and march Emperor Huizong, Emperor Qinzong, and roughly 3,000 members of the imperial court north into captivity — most never returned, and Huizong died in northern captivity in 1135.

Prince Zhao Gou, away from Kaifeng on a diplomatic mission when the city fell, fled south and re-established the Song dynasty as Emperor Gaozong; by 1138 the court had settled at Hangzhou, officially designated Lin'an or 'Temporarily at Peace.'

Hangzhou grew to over a million residents and became arguably the largest city in the world; Marco Polo, visiting in the 1270s, called it 'the finest and noblest city in the world' and counted twelve thousand bridges.
Yue Fei was a brilliant peasant-born Southern Song general whose mother had famously tattooed four characters on his back: 'Serve the country with utmost loyalty.'

Yue Fei won repeated victories against the Jurchen and was poised to recapture Kaifeng when Chancellor Qin Hui issued twelve gold-embossed imperial decrees in a single day recalling him to the capital, then had him executed in 1142 on fabricated charges.

Qin Hui's reply when asked what evidence existed for the charges — mo xu you, 'perhaps it exists' — became one of the most quoted phrases in Chinese political vocabulary, and visitors have spat on the iron statues of Qin Hui kneeling at Yue Fei's tomb in Hangzhou for nearly nine hundred years.

The Southern Song navy was China's first permanent standing navy, with paddle-wheel warships powered by foot-operated treadmills inside the hull — an engineering principle Europe would not reinvent until the 19th century steam paddle wheelers.

The Song held the Yangtze defensive line for over a century against the Jurchen Jin through a combination of natural barrier, technological advantages in gunpowder weaponry and naval engineering, and demographic-economic weight.

title: Song Society
bullets: Zhu Xi synthesized centuries of Confucian thought with Buddhist and Daoist metaphysics into Neo-Confucianism, built around li (principle) and qi (material force) and gewu ('investigating things') as the path to moral cultivation.

Zhu Xi's commentaries on the Four Books — Analects, Mencius, Great Learning, Doctrine of the Mean — became the official curriculum for Chinese civil service examinations from 1313 to 1905, a continuous run of 592 years that also shaped Korean, Japanese, and Vietnamese thought.

Foot-binding appears to have begun as a fashion among Song court dancers in the 10th or 11th century and spread gradually over the following centuries, becoming nearly universal among Han Chinese women by the late Qing — one of the most physically destructive cultural practices ever institutionalized at scale.

Male literacy in Northern Song urban areas may have reached 30–40% — a figure that would not be matched in Europe until the late 17th century, driven by cheap woodblock printing and the demand for examination-prep texts.

Li Qingzhao, who lost her husband and their famous art collection in the Jurchen catastrophe of 1127, is generally considered the greatest female poet in Chinese history; her ci poetry on grief and displacement is among the most directly devastating in Chinese literature.

Su Shi excelled simultaneously as ci poet, prose essayist, calligrapher, painter, statesman, and gastronome — the Song ideal of the cultivated gentleman-official; his Red Cliff Rhapsodies are among the most celebrated works in Chinese literature, and he is also credited with inventing Dongpo pork.

The Song court designated Jingdezhen as the official imperial porcelain supplier in 1004, beginning a thousand-year run during which Jingdezhen would be the global capital of porcelain production through the Ming and Qing dynasties.

Song multi-masted junks with watertight bulkhead compartments and the magnetic compass enabled an Indian Ocean trade network reaching East Africa; Song porcelain has been excavated at coastal sites in modern Kenya, Tanzania, and Mozambique.

Quanzhou — known to Arab traders as Zayton — was arguably the world's busiest port by the late 13th century, with foreign communities maintaining their own mosques, Hindu temples, and Nestorian Christian churches, and was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2021.

title: The Mongol Conquest of the Song
bullets: The Mongol Empire had absorbed China's northern neighbors with terrifying efficiency through the 13th century — destroying the Tangut Western Xia in 1227, the Jurchen Jin in 1234, and the Dali kingdom in Yunnan in 1253 — leaving the Southern Song as the holdout.

Kublai Khan formally proclaimed the Yuan dynasty in 1271, deliberately presenting himself as a legitimate Chinese emperor in the established dynastic succession rather than as a Mongol khan governing Chinese territory.

The decisive campaign was the six-year siege of Xiangyang from 1267 to 1273, which broke when the Mongols imported Muslim engineers Ismail and Ala al-Din from the Ilkhanate of Persia to build counterweight trebuchets capable of hurling 220-pound stones at the city walls.

These were the same counterweight trebuchet designs developed in the Crusader-Islamic interface of the 12th century — a Eurasian technology transfer that ran from the Mediterranean through the Mongol communications network all the way to the Yangtze.

Hangzhou surrendered in early 1276 under the regent dowager empress, who handed over the imperial seals along with the six-year-old Emperor Gong; the boy emperor and most of the court were sent to Beijing as captives.

Loyalist officials proclaimed two of the boy emperor's younger half-brothers in succession and continued resisting from coastal-fortress positions in modern Guangdong with the remnants of the Song navy.

On March 19, 1279, at Yamen in the Pearl River estuary, the trapped Song fleet was destroyed; Minister Lu Xiufu tied the eight-year-old Emperor Bing to his back and jumped into the sea, with reportedly more than 100,000 loyalists following them in collective suicide.

For the first time in Chinese history, all of China was under foreign rule; the Mongols inherited the most sophisticated economy, the most advanced bureaucracy, the largest urban population, the most innovative technology base, and the most globally connected commercial network on Earth.

The constitutional model the Song had pioneered — civilian bureaucratic rule with examination-selected officials, deliberately built to prevent the kind of military coups that had ended the Tang — would persist as the basic structure of Chinese governance until the abolition of the imperial examinations in 1905.
